Mental Health Risk Analysis

Kayla Lu

2026-03-07

Introduction

Mental health is influenced by multiple demographic, lifestyle,and work-related factors.

This project explores the distribution of mental health risk and examines how different factors such as gender, sleep, stress level, and work environment are associated with mental health outcomes.

Data Preparation

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

# Read dataset
mental_data <- read_csv("mental_health_dataset.csv")

Data Inspection

head(mental_data)
## # A tibble: 6 × 14
##     age gender     employment_status work_environment mental_health_history
##   <dbl> <chr>      <chr>             <chr>            <chr>                
## 1    56 Male       Employed          On-site          Yes                  
## 2    46 Female     Student           On-site          No                   
## 3    32 Female     Employed          On-site          Yes                  
## 4    60 Non-binary Self-employed     On-site          No                   
## 5    25 Female     Self-employed     On-site          Yes                  
## 6    38 Female     Unemployed        On-site          Yes                  
## # ℹ 9 more variables: seeks_treatment <chr>, stress_level <dbl>,
## #   sleep_hours <dbl>, physical_activity_days <dbl>, depression_score <dbl>,
## #   anxiety_score <dbl>, social_support_score <dbl>, productivity_score <dbl>,
## #   mental_health_risk <chr>
str(mental_data)
## spc_tbl_ [10,000 × 14] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ age                   : num [1:10000] 56 46 32 60 25 38 56 36 40 28 ...
##  $ gender                : chr [1:10000] "Male" "Female" "Female" "Non-binary" ...
##  $ employment_status     : chr [1:10000] "Employed" "Student" "Employed" "Self-employed" ...
##  $ work_environment      : chr [1:10000] "On-site" "On-site" "On-site" "On-site" ...
##  $ mental_health_history : chr [1:10000] "Yes" "No" "Yes" "No" ...
##  $ seeks_treatment       : chr [1:10000] "Yes" "Yes" "No" "No" ...
##  $ stress_level          : num [1:10000] 6 10 7 4 3 3 2 8 7 8 ...
##  $ sleep_hours           : num [1:10000] 6.2 9 7.7 4.5 5.4 9.9 5.5 7.1 6.5 3.1 ...
##  $ physical_activity_days: num [1:10000] 3 4 2 4 0 3 1 5 6 0 ...
##  $ depression_score      : num [1:10000] 28 30 24 6 24 17 25 25 28 7 ...
##  $ anxiety_score         : num [1:10000] 17 11 7 0 12 9 3 8 6 18 ...
##  $ social_support_score  : num [1:10000] 54 85 62 95 70 63 87 72 46 88 ...
##  $ productivity_score    : num [1:10000] 59.7 54.9 61.3 97 69 69.3 63.1 58.4 60.4 93.2 ...
##  $ mental_health_risk    : chr [1:10000] "High" "High" "Medium" "Low" ...

##   .. cols(

##   ..   employment_status = col_character(),
##   ..   work_environment = col_character(),
##   ..   mental_health_history = col_character(),
##   ..   seeks_treatment = col_character(),
##   ..   stress_level = col_double(),
##   ..   sleep_hours = col_double(),
##   ..   physical_activity_days = col_double(),
##   ..   depression_score = col_double(),
##   ..   anxiety_score = col_double(),
##   ..   social_support_score = col_double(),
##   ..   productivity_score = col_double(),
##   ..   mental_health_risk = col_character()
##   .. )
##  - attr(*, "problems")=<externalptr>
summary(mental_data)
##       age           gender          employment_status  work_environment  
##  Min.   :18.00   Length:10000       Length:10000       Length:10000      
##  1st Qu.:30.00   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Median :41.50   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  Mean   :41.56                                                           
##  3rd Qu.:53.00                                                           
##  Max.   :65.00                                                           
##  mental_health_history seeks_treatment     stress_level     sleep_hours    
##  Length:10000          Length:10000       Min.   : 1.000   Min.   : 3.000  
##  Class :character      Class :character   1st Qu.: 3.000   1st Qu.: 5.500  
##  Mode  :character      Mode  :character   Median : 6.000   Median : 6.500  
##                                           Mean   : 5.572   Mean   : 6.473  
##                                           3rd Qu.: 8.000   3rd Qu.: 7.500  
##                                           Max.   :10.000   Max.   :10.000  
##  physical_activity_days depression_score anxiety_score   social_support_score
##  Min.   :0.000          Min.   : 0.00    Min.   : 0.00   Min.   :  0.00      
##  1st Qu.:2.000          1st Qu.: 7.00    1st Qu.: 5.00   1st Qu.: 25.00      
##  Median :4.000          Median :15.00    Median :11.00   Median : 50.00      
##  Mean   :3.506          Mean   :15.04    Mean   :10.56   Mean   : 50.12      
##  3rd Qu.:5.000          3rd Qu.:23.00    3rd Qu.:16.00   3rd Qu.: 76.00      
##  Max.   :7.000          Max.   :30.00    Max.   :21.00   Max.   :100.00      
##  productivity_score mental_health_risk
##  Min.   : 42.80     Length:10000      
##  1st Qu.: 65.80     Class :character  
##  Median : 77.60     Mode  :character  
##  Mean   : 77.31                       
##  3rd Qu.: 89.20                       
##  Max.   :100.00
colSums(is.na(mental_data))
##                    age                 gender      employment_status 
##                      0                      0                      0 
##       work_environment  mental_health_history        seeks_treatment 
##                      0                      0                      0 
##           stress_level            sleep_hours physical_activity_days 
##                      0                      0                      0 
##       depression_score          anxiety_score   social_support_score 
##                      0                      0                      0 
##     productivity_score     mental_health_risk 
##                      0                      0

Data Cleaning

mental_data$gender <- as.factor(mental_data$gender)
mental_data$employment_status <- as.factor(mental_data$employment_status)
mental_data$work_environment <- as.factor(mental_data$work_environment)
mental_data$mental_health_history <- as.factor(mental_data$mental_health_history)
mental_data$seeks_treatment <- as.factor(mental_data$seeks_treatment)

mental_data$mental_health_risk <- factor(
  mental_data$mental_health_risk,
  levels = c("Low","Medium","High")
)

Data Visualisations and Analysis

Sample Overview

Age Distribution

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill="skyblue", color="white") +
  labs (title="Age Distribusion",
        x= "Age", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

The age distribution shows that most participants fall within the young to middle-aged adult range, suggesting that the dataset primarily represents working-age individuals.

Gender Distribution

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x=gender)) +
         geom_bar(fill="skyblue") +
         theme_minimal() +
         labs(title="Gender")

The dataset includes participants from different gender groups, with gender groups fairly evenly represented

Core Analysis

Define custom colors

risk_colors <- c(
  "Low" = "lightgreen",
  "Medium" = "khaki",
  "High" = "lightcoral"
)
scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors)
## <ggproto object: Class ScaleDiscrete, Scale, gg>
##     aesthetics: fill
##     axis_order: function
##     break_info: function
##     break_positions: function
##     breaks: waiver
##     call: call
##     clone: function
##     dimension: function
##     drop: TRUE
##     expand: waiver
##     fallback_palette: function
##     get_breaks: function
##     get_breaks_minor: function
##     get_labels: function
##     get_limits: function
##     get_transformation: function
##     guide: legend
##     is_discrete: function
##     is_empty: function
##     labels: waiver
##     limits: function
##     make_sec_title: function
##     make_title: function
##     map: function
##     map_df: function
##     minor_breaks: waiver
##     n.breaks.cache: NULL
##     na.translate: TRUE
##     na.value: grey50
##     name: waiver
##     palette: function
##     palette.cache: NULL
##     position: left
##     range: environment
##     rescale: function
##     reset: function
##     train: function
##     train_df: function
##     transform: function
##     transform_df: function
##     super:  <ggproto object: Class ScaleDiscrete, Scale, gg>

1. Mental health Risk Distribution

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, fill= mental_health_risk)) + 
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +   
  geom_text(stat = "count",
    aes(label = ..count..),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Mental Health Risk",
    x = "Mental helath Risk", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 
## Warning: The dot-dot notation (`..count..`) was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `after_stat(count)` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

The distribution of mental health risk varies across participants, with most individuals falling into the Medium risk category.

2. Gender vs Mental Health Risk

Gendaer_mental_porportion <- mental_data %>%
  count(mental_health_risk, gender) %>%
  group_by(gender) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))

ggplot(Gendaer_mental_porportion, aes(x = gender, y = prop, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +  
  labs(title = "Gender vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Gender", y="Percent") +
  theme_minimal()

Mental health risk is similarly distributed across genders, with most participants at medium risk, then high, and the fewest at low risk.

Mental Health Measures

3. Depression vs Mental Health Risk

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = depression_score, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +  
  labs(title = "Depresion score vs Mental Helath Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y = "Depresion Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Depression scores are clearly associated with mental health risk: high scores correspond to high risk, medium scores to medium risk, and low scores to low risk.

4. Anxiety vs Mental Health Risk

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y= anxiety_score, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +  
  labs(title = "Anxiety score vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y ="Anxiety Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Anxiety scores are also associated with mental health risk, with higher scores indicating greater risk, moderate scores corresponding to medium risk, and lower scores to low risk.

5. Seek treatment vs Mental health Risk

Seek_treatment_mental_porportion <- mental_data %>%
  count(seeks_treatment, mental_health_risk) %>%
  group_by(mental_health_risk) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))

ggplot(Seek_treatment_mental_porportion, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = prop, fill = seeks_treatment)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Proportion of Seeking Treatment by Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health History", y = "Propotion", fill = "Seek Treatment") +
  theme_minimal()

Across all three mental health risk levels, the majority of participants have not sought treatment; however, the high-risk group has a slightly higher proportion of participants who have sought treatment compared to the other two groups.

6. Mental Health History vs Mental health Risk

History_proportion <- mental_data %>%
  count(mental_health_history, mental_health_risk) %>%
  group_by(mental_health_risk) %>%
  mutate (prop = n / sum(n))
  
ggplot(History_proportion, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = prop, fill = mental_health_history)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Proportion of Mental Health History by Mental health Risk",
       x  ="Mental Helath Risk", y = "Proportion" , fill = "Mental Health History") +
  theme_minimal()

The distribution of mental health history is similar across all three risk levels, with about two-thirds of participants reporting no history. The high-risk group has a slightly higher proportion of participants with no history compared to the other two groups.

Health and Lifestyle Factors

7. Sleep vs Mental Health Risk

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = sleep_hours, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +
  labs(title = "Sleep hours vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y = "Sleep Hours") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Sleep duration is similarly distributed across all three mental health risk levels, with most participants sleeping between 5.5 and 7.5 hours.

8. Stress Level

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = stress_level, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +  
  labs(title = "Stress Level vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y = "Stress Level") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

The overall range of stress levels is consistent across all three risk levels, with median scores being notably higher for medium and high-risk participants.

9. Social Support vs Mental Health Risk

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = social_support_score, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +
  labs(title = "Social Support Score vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y ="Social Support Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Social support scores are similar across all mental health risk levels.

Work-Related Factors

10. Employment Status vs Menatl Health Risk

Employment_mental_propotion <- mental_data %>%
  count(employment_status, mental_health_risk) %>%
  group_by(mental_health_risk) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))

ggplot(Employment_mental_propotion, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y= prop, fill = employment_status)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Proportion of Emploment Status by Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y = "Proportion") +
  theme_minimal()

Employment status is generally similar across all mental health risk levels, with most participants being employed, followed by students, and the fewest unemployed or self-employed. In the high-risk group, the proportion of employed and students is slightly higher than in the other two groups.

11. Work Environment vs Mental Health Risk

Environment_mental_proportion <- mental_data %>%
  count(work_environment, mental_health_risk) %>%
  group_by(mental_health_risk) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))

ggplot(Environment_mental_proportion, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = prop, fill= work_environment))+
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Proportion of Work Environment by Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y = "Propotion") +
  theme_minimal()

Overall, most participants work onsite, accounting for nearly half, followed by remote and hybrid work. However, in the high-risk group, the proportion of onsite workers is lower than in the other two groups.

12. Productivity vs Mental Health Risk

ggplot(mental_data, aes(x = mental_health_risk, y = productivity_score, fill = mental_health_risk)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = risk_colors) +
  labs(title = "Productivity Score vs Mental Health Risk",
       x = "Mental Health Risk", y ="Productivity Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

The productivity scores show a distinct downward trend across the three risk levels. The median score drops noticeably from low to high risk, and the middle 50% of scores show almost no overlap between risk levels.

Conclusion

This analysis explored demographic, lifestyle, and work-related factors associated with mental health risk.

The results suggest that higher depression scores, anxiety scores, and stress levels are associated with increased mental health risk. Additionally, there is a negative relationship between mental health risk and productivity scores, indicating that participants with higher mental health risk tend to have lower productivity. This analysis explored demographic and lifestyle factors associated with mental health risk.

Overall, these findings highlight that psychological wellbeing is the main driver of mental health risk, while productivity is inversely related. Addressing psychological factors may help improve both mental health outcomes and work performance.
